narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 53.4% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use to make admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, test scores, family background, and enrollment plans, focusing on patterns seen in past students without considering gender or race. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 grade decile of 1, which is much lower than the average of about 6 for students who passed the bar, signaling to the model a potential struggle with sustained performance. Counterbalancing that somewhat was your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, which exceeds the 3.24 average among bar passers and nudged the prediction toward success. Your upper-middle family income also helped in a positive direction, as it aligns closely with the bracket held by nearly half of those who passed, suggesting some supportive advantages. Compared to typical admitted students—who often have higher deciles in later years, strong LSAT scores around 37, and full-time enrollment plans like yours—your lower Year 1 and Year 3 deciles and LSAT of 26 stood out as bigger hurdles, even with your solid undergrad grades. While your full-time status matches most successful students, it wasn't enough to offset the top concerns. This close call at 53.4% means the model saw real risks, but also strengths that kept it from being a sure rejection. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the passing average, as that's a key area where you lag behind admits. Strengthening your recent academic decile through additional coursework or grad school could also help rebuild that signal. Reach out to admissions for feedback on improving your profile, and explore programs with bridge courses or bar prep support tailored for students like you. With targeted steps, you can shift these predictions in your favor for future applications.
